question.text: A life insurance policy taken under Section 6 of the Married Women's Property Act (MWPA) has which of the following characteristics?

answer.text: It creates a trust in favour of the wife and/or children, and the policy proceeds cannot be attached by any court for the debts of the insured
It allows the insured to change the beneficiary at any time and the proceeds can be used to settle outstanding debts of the insured
It provides tax-free maturity benefits but the death benefit is taxable under the Income Tax Act
It is available only for policies with a sum assured above Rs. 50 lakhs and requires annual renewal of the trust deed

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Section 6 of the MWPA mandates that a life insurance policy taken under this act creates a statutory trust. The beneficiaries (wife and/or children) have a direct claim on the proceeds. Critically, these proceeds are ring-fenced from creditors -- no court can attach them for the insured's debts. **The Trap:** The ability to "change beneficiary at any time" is incorrect -- MWPA policies have irrevocable beneficiary designations. There is no minimum sum assured requirement and no annual renewal of the trust deed. **Cross-Exam Context:** MWPA is tested in NISM X-B as a key estate planning and creditor-protection tool that investment advisers must understand. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** MWPA policies are essential for business owners and professionals with potential liability exposure. The creditor protection makes them superior to regular policies for family financial security.
